title: Transformer Models Compared: BERT vs GPT vs T5 Guide

title: Transformer Models: GPT, BERT, T5 Overview and Applications
snippet: The transformer architecture has proven remarkably versatile and powerful, forming the foundation for most contemporary natural language processing systems. Understanding the differences between major model families like GPT, BERT, and T5 provides crucial context for selecting and applying these technologies effectively.
content: Transformer Models: GPT, BERT, T5 Overview and Applications Skip to content KNOWBO TECHNOLOGIES Agentic AI for the Enterprise World Menu About Us Privacy Policy Terms and Conditions Courses SAP BTP Administration Fundamentals Prompt Engineering Generative AI & RAG System Administration AWS Artificial Intelligence Agentic AI Systems Books SAP BTPAdministrationFrom Zero to Hero Shop Cart Checkout Contact Us My account Overview of transformer models (GPT, BERT, T5, etc.) Posted on Oct 6, 2025 Home Generative AI & RAG Course Overview of transformer models (GPT, BERT, T5, etc.) The Transformer Revolution: Redefining AI Architecture Transformer models have fundamentally reshaped the artificial intelligence landscape since their introduction in Google’s groundbreaking 2017 paper “Attention Is All You Need.” These architectures replaced earlier recurrent and convolutional approaches with a self-attention mechanism that processes all elements in a sequence simultaneously while understanding their contextual relationships. This parallel processing capability dramatically improved training efficiency and enabled models to handle much longer text sequences than previous architectures. The transformer’s scalability and effectiveness across diverse tasks sparked an explosion of innovation that continues to accelerate today. What makes transformer models particularly revolutionary is their ability to capture long-range dependencies in data without the sequential processing limitations of earlier approaches. Where RNNs and LSTMs struggled with maintaining context across lengthy passages, transformers can effortlessly relate words at the beginning of a document to those at the end. This architectural advantage, combined with massive scaling in model size and training data, has enabled the development of models with unprecedented language understanding and generation capabilities. The transformer foundation now underpins virtually all state-of-the-art natural language processing systems. Core Transformer Architecture: Understanding the Building Blocks The transformer architecture consists of several key components that work together to process and understand sequential data. The self-attention mechanism forms the heart of the system, allowing each position in the sequence to attend to all other positions, computing weighted sums that represent contextual relationships. Multi-head attention expands this capability by running multiple self-attention operations in parallel, enabling the model to focus on different types of relationships simultaneously. Positional encoding provides information about word order since the transformer processes all words concurrently rather than sequentially. The encoder-decoder structure represents another fundamental aspect of the original transformer design. The encoder processes input sequences to create rich contextual representations, while the decoder generates output sequences based on these representations and previously generated tokens. Feed-forward networks within each layer apply nonlinear transformations to attention outputs. Layer normalization and residual connections stabilize training and enable the construction of very deep networks. These components combine to create a highly parallelizable architecture that scales efficiently with increased computational resources. Self-Attention Mechanism: The Heart of Transformers The self-attention mechanism represents the transformational innovation that distinguishes transformers from previous architectures. This mechanism works by creating three vectors for each input word: Query, Key, and Value. The Query vector represents what the word is looking for, the Key vector represents what information the word contains, and the Value vector represents the actual content the word contributes. The model computes attention scores by taking the dot product of Query vectors with Key vectors, then applies softmax to create probability distributions that determine how much focus to place on other words when encoding a particular word. This attention process allows the model to dynamically determine which parts of the input sequence are most relevant for understanding each word. For example, when processing the word “bank” in different contexts, the model might attend strongly to “river” in one case and “money” in another, resolving the ambiguity based on surrounding context. The multi-head aspect enables the model to simultaneously attend to information from different representation subspaces, capturing various types of syntactic, semantic, and pragmatic relationships within the same processing step. GPT Models: Autoregressive Language Generation The Generative Pre-trained Transformer (GPT) series from OpenAI exemplifies the decoder-only transformer approach optimized for text generation. These models employ a left-to-right autoregressive training objective where they predict each next word based on all previous words in the sequence. This training methodology enables powerful text generation capabilities but means the models can only attend to previous context, not future words. The GPT architecture stacks multiple transformer decoder blocks, each containing masked self-attention and feed-forward layers. GPT models have evolved through multiple generations, with each version demonstrating dramatic improvements in capability. GPT-1 introduced the concept of generative pre-training followed by task-specific fine-tuning. GPT-2 scaled this approach significantly and demonstrated impressive few-shot learning capabilities. GPT-3 achieved remarkable performance through massive scaling to 175 billion parameters, showing that large language models could perform many tasks without explicit fine-tuning. Subsequent versions have continued pushing boundaries in reasoning, instruction following, and specialized domain performance. GPT Training Methodology and Capabilities GPT models train using a simple yet powerful self-supervised objective: predicting the next word in a sequence given all previous words. This approach leverages massive unlabeled text corpora from the internet, books, and other sources, allowing the models to develop broad world knowledge and language understanding. The pre-training phase creates a foundation model that can then be adapted to specific tasks through fine-tuning, prompt engineering, or in-context learning. The scalability of this approach has proven remarkably consistent, with larger models and more training data consistently improving performance across diverse tasks. The capabilities emerging from GPT-style training include coherent long-form text generation, question answering, summarization, translation, and code generation. These models demonstrate impressive few-shot and zero-shot learning, adapting to new tasks from just a few examples or simple instructions. Their generative nature makes them particularly valuable for creative applications, content creation, and conversational interfaces. However, their autoregressive design also introduces challenges including tendency toward repetition, factual inconsistencies, and difficulty with tasks requiring look-ahead planning. BERT Models: Bidirectional Context Understanding Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) introduced a fundamentally different approach from GPT by leveraging bidirectional context during pre-training. Developed by Google, BERT uses a masked language modeling objective where random words in the input sequence are replaced with [MASK] tokens, and the model must predict the original words based on surrounding context from both directions. This bidirectional understanding enables richer contextual representations particularly valuable for understanding tasks rather than generation tasks. The BERT architecture consists of transformer encoder blocks that process entire sequences simultaneously, allowing each word to att

title: AI Model Comparisons - GPT vs. BERT vs. LLaMA, and other ML models.
snippet: This article compares GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer), BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers), and LLaMA (Large Language Model Meta AI), along with other popular ML models, to help understand their differences, use cases, and performance.
content: AI Model Comparisons – GPT vs. BERT vs. LLaMA, and other ML models. Skip to main content Search This Blog Artificial intelligence (AI) AI Model Comparisons – GPT vs. BERT vs. LLaMA, and other ML models. Get link Facebook X Pinterest Email Other Apps March 13, 2025 Introduction Artificial Intelligence (AI) has seen significant advancements with the development of powerful machine learning (ML) models. Among them, GPT, BERT, and LLaMA have emerged as some of the most influential models, each with unique strengths and applications. This article compares GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer), BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers), and LLaMA (Large Language Model Meta AI) , along with other popular ML models, to help understand their differences, use cases, and performance. 1. Overview of AI Models Model Developer Key Function GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) | OpenAI | Text generation and completion BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) | Google | NLP tasks like search ranking, Q&A, sentiment analysis LLaMA (Large Language Model Meta AI) | Meta (Facebook) | Efficient, open-source text processing T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer) | Google | Multi-task NLP model XLNet | Google/CMU | Improved BERT-style model for NLP tasks Claude | Anthropic | AI chatbot optimized for safe and useful interactions PaLM (Pathways Language Model) | Google | Scalable and efficient language model 2. GPT vs. BERT vs. LLaMA – Key Differences 🔹 1. GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) ✅ Developed by OpenAI ✅ Focuses on text generation ✅ Unidirectional (left-to-right text prediction) ✅ Used for chatbots, creative writing, and content generation ✅ Examples: ChatGPT, GPT-4, GPT-3.5 🔹 2. BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) ✅ Developed by Google ✅ Focuses on understanding context in text ✅ Bidirectional (analyzes words before & after the target word) ✅ Used for search engines, Q&A systems, and text classification ✅ Examples: Google Search, Google Assistant NLP 🔹 3. LLaMA (Large Language Model Meta AI) ✅ Developed by Meta (Facebook) ✅ Focuses on efficient NLP for research and open-source AI ✅ Smaller yet highly efficient model compared to GPT ✅ Used for academic AI research and NLP applications ✅ Examples: LLaMA 2, LLaMA 3 (upcoming) 3. Performance & Use Cases Feature GPT (OpenAI) BERT (Google) LLaMA (Meta) Primary Use Case | Text Generation | NLP Understanding | Efficient NLP Processing Training Approach | Pre-trained & Fine-tuned | Pre-trained, Contextual | Pre-trained, Lightweight Best for | Chatbots, Story Writing, AI Assistants | Search Engine Queries, Sentiment Analysis | Research, Open-source AI Models Directionality | Unidirectional | Bidirectional | Bidirectional Model Size | Large (billions of parameters) | Medium (optimized for context) | Smaller but efficient 4. Other Notable AI Models 🔹 T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer) ✅ Developed by Google ✅ Converts all NLP tasks into a text-to-text format ✅ Used for translation, summarization, question answering 🔹 XLNet ✅ Developed by Google & CMU ✅ Improves on BERT’s bidirectional training approach ✅ Used for NLP understanding, text classification, and ranking 🔹 Claude (Anthropic) ✅ Developed by Anthropic ✅ Focuses on safe and human-aligned AI conversations ✅ Used for chatbots, productivity tools, and AI research 🔹 PaLM (Pathways Language Model) ✅ Developed by Google ✅ Uses a scalable architecture for multiple AI tasks ✅ Used for code generation, complex reasoning, and multimodal AI 5. Future Trends in AI Model Development 🔹 What’s Next for AI Models? ✅ Multimodal AI – Models that integrate text, images, and audio. ✅ Smaller, More Efficient Models – AI that requires fewer resources but delivers high performance. ✅ Open-Source AI Models – More transparency and accessibility for researchers. ✅ AI Safety & Ethics – Improved AI alignment to avoid bias and misuse. Conclusion Understanding the differences between GPT, BERT, LLaMA, and other ML models helps in selecting the right AI for specific use cases. GPT excels in text generation, BERT in language understanding, and LLaMA in efficient NLP processing. Meanwhile, models like T5, XLNet, and PaLM continue to push the boundaries of AI capabilities. AI Model Comparison Summary Model Best Use Case GPT | Chatbots, AI writing assistants BERT | Search ranking, NLP understanding LLaMA | Efficient open-source AI processing T5 | Multi-task NLP applications XLNet | Advanced language modeling Claude | AI-driven human interactions PaLM | Large-scale reasoning & multimodal AI 🚀 The AI landscape is evolving rapidly—choose the right model based on your needs! Get link Facebook X Pinterest Email Other Apps Comments Post a Comment Popular posts from this blog AI in Game Development – AI-based NPCs, game logic, and procedural generation. March 09, 2025   Introduction Artificial Intelligence (AI) is revolutionizing game development by enhancing non-player characters (NPCs), game logic, and procedural generation . AI-driven game mechanics create immersive experiences, making games more realistic, dynamic, and adaptive to player behavior. This guide explores the role of AI in game development and how it’s transforming the industry. 1. AI-Powered NPCs (Non-Player Characters) What Are AI NPCs? AI NPCs are characters in a game that behave dynamically, adapting to player actions rather than following pre-defined scripts. Techniques Used: Finite State Machines (FSMs) : Used in classic games for structured AI behavior (e.g., enemy patrol, attack, flee states). Behavior Trees (BTs) : A more advanced AI model that determines NPC decisions based on conditions. Machine Learning (ML) & Reinforcement Learning (RL) : Allows NPCs to learn from gameplay and evolve their behavior. Example: Implementing AI in an NPC (Python + Unity)... Read more Custom AI Model Training – How to fine-tune AI models for specific tasks. March 13, 2025   Introduction Training a custom AI model allows businesses and researchers to adapt machine learning (ML) models for specific tasks , such as text analysis, image recognition, fraud detection, or personalized recommendations. Fine-tuning pre-trained AI models can enhance accuracy, reduce training time, and optimize performance for domain-specific applications. This guide will walk you through the steps, tools, and best practices for fine-tuning AI models based on different use cases. 1. Understanding Fine-Tuning in AI 🔹 What is Fine-Tuning? Fine-tuning refers to the process of taking a pre-trained model and adapting it to a specific task by training it further on domain-specific data. This allows models to leverage prior learning while being customized for particular needs. ✅ Why Fine-Tune Instead of Training from Scratch? Saves computational resources Requires less labeled data Improves performance on specialized tasks Reduces training time 🔹 Types of AI Model... title: GPT vs. BERT vs. LLaMA: Which Transformer Model Should You Use?
snippet: Final Takeaway Use GPT/LLaMA if you want a chatbot, content generator, or reasoning model. Use BERT if you want classification, embeddings, or search ranking.
content: GPT vs. BERT vs. LLaMA: Which Transformer Model Should You Use? | by Maheera Amjad | Medium Sitemap Open in app Sign up Sign in Medium Logo Get app Write Search Sign up Sign in Member-only story GPT vs. BERT vs. LLaMA: Which Transformer Model Should You Use? Comparing the strengths and best use cases of leading LLMs Maheera Amjad 5 min read · Oct 24, 2025 -- Share Press enter or click to view image in full size Not all Transformer models are built for the same purpose. Understanding their core architecture is the key to master LLM. At the core, a Transformer architecture is composed of Encoder and Decoder, where both of their own strength. Based on the utilisation of these fundamental blocks, Transformer models vary from each other. Model — Architectural Class — Attention Mechanism — Context Flow — Primary Strength GPT — Decoder-Only — Causal (Masked) Attention — Unidirectional (Left-to-Right) — Generation (Creativity, Flow) BERT — Encoder-Only — Standard (Unmasked) Attention — Bidirectional (Left↔Right) — Understanding (Context, Precision) LLaMA — Decoder-Only — Causal (Masked) Attention — Unidirectional (Left-to-Right) — Efficient Generation & Open-Source GPT: The Creative Generator (Decoder-Only) The Generative Pre-trained Transformer models (GPT-3, GPT-4, etc.) are built on a decoder-only architecture. It uses masking mechanism, which allows the model to only see the words before the current one and predict the next. This creates autoregression, predicting one… -- -- Written by Maheera Amjad 256 followers · 600 following PhD scholar | Bioinformatician | Writer | Researcher | Machine Learning | Genomics | Transcriptomics | Sequence Analysis | Scientist No responses yet Help Status About Careers Press Blog Privacy Rules Terms Text to speech

title: BERT, GPT, and T5 — the transformer brothers - Medium
snippet: T5 seems handy for text-generation tasks, but what is precisely different between using T5 compared to the other popular transformer-based models, such as BERT and GPT?
content: BERT, GPT, and T5 — the transformer brothers | by Zhihan Lu | Medium Sitemap Open in app Sign up Sign in Medium Logo Get app Write Search Sign up Sign in BERT, GPT, and T5 — the transformer brothers Zhihan Lu 6 min read · Jan 10, 2023 -- Listen Share How are they different — and when to use them? During my Master’s degree at CMU, I took a Natural Language Process course. In the class, my team worked on a project that uses T5 to do data-to-text generation. T5 seems handy for text-generation tasks, but what is precisely different between using T5 compared to the other popular transformer-based models, such as BERT and GPT? I will summarize the differences between each of these three Transformer-based models in this article. Transformer Revisited In order to understand BERT, GPT, T5, and their differences, we first need to take a look at the Transformer model. Below is the illustration of a Transformer model, possibly the most widely used illustration in the history of Machine Learning: Figure 1: the Transformer ( source ) The Transformer architecture has two parts: the encoder on the left side of Figure 1 and the decoder on the right side of Figure 1. Below are the two main differences between these two parts of the architecture: For the encoder, the multi-head attention is not masked. This means the output of any token depends on the entire input sequence. On the other hand, the multi-head attention for the decoder is masked . An output token can only attend to the previous outputs, not the ones that come after it. For the output layer, the encoder directly outputs an H-dimensional embedding vector for each input token. The decoder, on the other hand, maps the embedding vectors into a V dimensional vector through a fully connected layer, where V is the size of the output vocabulary. Then, through a softmax transformation, the decoder selects the next output token with argmax. This token will be concatenated with the previous output tokens and fed into the next round of decoder computation to again generate the next token. To summarize, the Transformer decoder is used to generate a sequence of tokens one by one, while the encoder is used to generate embeddings. This has great implications for the design and the use cases of BERT, GPT, and T5. BERT: the encoder Figure 2: BERT ( source ) In terms of architecture, BERT is the Transformer encoder. This has two implications, corresponding to the two key differences between the encoder and decoder outlined above: There is no way for BERT to do normal language modeling. The attention in the encoder will attend to the entire sequence and therefore cannot achieve things like next-word prediction over the entire sequence. This actually motivates BERT developers to “mask out” words in the middle of the sentence so that the model can be trained. The output of BERT is a batch of token embeddings with hidden size H=768/1024, one for each input token. Therefore, for downstream tasks such as next-sentence prediction, the BERT paper needs to attach a [CLS] token at the start and use its output embedding for classification. Because BERT is only an encoder and lacks the ability for text generation, the original paper selects tasks such as span prediction (SQuAD), classification (the GLUE benchmark), and multiple choice (SWAG). This shows that BERT may not be suitable for text generation tasks, but instead is very good at language embedding and classification tasks. Wait a moment! More advanced readers may have already started doubting the conclusion. Indeed, many people have used BERT embeddings as inputs for downstream text generators ( here is an example that uses LSTM). There are also studies showing that BERT itself is perfectly capable of text generation! Here is the interesting paper for reference. The takeaway is that, although BERT is not originally designed for text generation, it can find its way into many generation frameworks by providing the essential embeddings to the generator. GPT: the decoder Figure 3: GPT ( source ) While BERT is a Transformer encoder, GPT is a decoder. This brings two corresponding implications to GPT’s approach. The rationale behind GPT — the “generative pertaining transformers” — is all about language modeling, or “generative pretraining”. The task suits the decoder structure as it is a natural fit for language modeling through next token prediction. This is probably why GPT uses the decoder only, as that is what is indeed needed for text generation. If GPT uses the original form of the transformer decoder, it cannot take a paragraph of text as input easily. Therefore, the GPT team tweaks the decoder’s use case. In order to do tasks with text input, they use the text as context and simply let the decoder generate the output embeddings. The final output layers are also changed so that, instead of obtaining a distribution over the output tokens, we can obtain a distribution over the target classes. Below is a figure from the original paper showing the format of the context text that is fed into the decoder to obtain the embeddings: Press enter or click to view image in full size Figure 4: GPT-1 tasks ( source ) Just as BERT can help with text generation, GPT is able to perform really well on the classification tasks, and even being able to surpass fine-tuned BERT in the few-shot setting ( source ). Yet ChatGPT reminded us once again how amazing the transformer decoder can generate text. The takeaway is that GPT is versatile enough to do both text embedding and text generation, although it is most natural to use it for generation tasks. T5: both the encoder and the decoder T5 — the Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer — the name itself shows why it needs both the encoder and the decoder! Unlike GPT which trains on text generation or BERT which trains on token-level prediction (such as MLM), T5 trains on text-to-text transfer. With only a few tweaks such as removing the Layer Norm bias, placing the layer normalization outside the residual path, and using a different position embedding scheme, T5 largely inherits the original Transformer architecture as it is. As the architecture is for text-to-text generation, T5 is trained on such tasks. Researchers designed a span-corruption objective. Specifically, 15% of the original sequence is corrupted and a single unique mask token is used to replace the entire span. The target text is then the original, uncorrupted text itself. For the downstream tasks, researchers also framed them in the form of text-to-text transfer. Below is an illustration of some of the tasks originally mentioned in the paper: Press enter or click to view image in full size Figure 5: downstream tasks for T5 as text-to-text generation ( source ) Another interesting example is how the T5 team transforms classification tasks such as those from GLUE and SuperGLUE into text-to-text generation tasks. For instance, in order to solve the co-reference task, T5 will be used to generate text for the pronoun in question. If the generated text is within the candidate noun phrase, the model will output True and otherwise False. For the training example “Text: Mark told Pete many lies about himself, which Pete included in his book. * He* should have been more truthful. Coreference: False”, if given the text with “He” marked by*, T5 model outputs “Pete”, then its prediction will be True; if the model outputs “Mark”, then the prediction will be False. Although T5 is designed for text-to-text transfer, it is able to become the SOTA on many other tasks such as GLUE, SuperGLUE, and SQuAD ( source ). Isn’t that amazing? In summary, BERT is for text/token embedding, GPT is for text generation, and T5 is for text-to-text transfer. This is what these models are designed for and are most naturally good at. However, any of these models are versatile enough for other downstream tasks, and we can tweak the input and downstream models to enable such use cases. Naturallanguageprocessing NLP Bert Gpt 3 Ch

If you want to contribute to this list, send a pull request or reach out to me on twitter: @abacaj . Let's make this list useful. There are a number of models available that are not entirely open source (non-commercial, etc), this repository should serve to also make you aware of that. Tracking the original source/company of the model will help. I would also eventually like to add model use cases. So it is easier for others to find the right one to fine-tune. title: Transformer models: an introduction and catalog - arXiv.org
snippet: Extension: Megatron is a family of models that extend previously known architectures (namely GPT-2 and BERT originally, but also T5 more recently) by introducing model parallelism primitives.
content: Transformer models: an introduction and catalog 1 Introduction: What are Transformers 1.1 Encoder/Decoder architecture 1.2 Attention 1.3 Foundation vs Fine-tuned models 1.4 The Impact of Transformers 1.5 A Note on Diffusion models 2 The Transformers Catalog 2.1 Features of a Transformer 2.1.1 Pretraining Architecture Encoder Pretraining Decoder Pretraining Transformer (Encoder-Decoder) Pretraining 2.1.2 Pretraining or Finetuning Task 2.1.3 Application 2.2 Catalog table 2.3 Family Tree 2.4 Chronological timeline A Catalog List A.1 ALBERT A.2 AlexaTM 20B A.3 Alpaca A.4 AlphaFold A.5 Anthropic Assistant A.6 BART A.7 BERT A.8 Big Bird A.9 BlenderBot3 A.10 BLOOM A.11 ChatGPT A.12 Chinchilla A.13 CLIP A.14 CM3 A.15 CTRL A.16 DALL-E A.17 DALL-E 2 A.18 DeBERTa A.19 Decision Transformers A.20 DialoGPT A.21 DistilBERT A.22 DQ-BART A.23 Dolly A.24 E5 A.25 ELECTRA A.26 ERNIE A.27 Flamingo A.28 Flan-T5 A.29 Flan-PaLM A.30 Galactica A.31 Gato A.32 GLaM A.33 GLIDE A.34 GLM A.35 Global Context ViT A.36 Gopher A.37 GopherCite A.38 GPT A.39 GPT-2 A.40 GPT-3 A.41 GPT-3.5 A.42 GPT-J A.43 GPT-Neo A.44 GPT-NeoX-20B A.45 HTLM A.46 Imagen A.47 InstructGPT A.48 InstructOR A.49 Jurassic-1 A.50 LAMDA A.51 LLaMA A.52 mBART A.53 Megatron A.54 Minerva A.55 MT-NLG (Megatron TuringNLG) A.56 OpenAssistant LLaMa A.57 OPT A.58 PalM A.59 Pegasus A.60 Pythia A.61 RoBERTa A.62 SeeKer A.63 Sparrow A.64 StableDiffusion A.65 Swin Transformer A.66 Switch A.67 T0 A.68 T5 A.69 Trajectory Transformers A.70 Transformer XL A.71 Turing-NLG A.72 UL2 A.73 Vicuna A.74 ViT A.75 Wu Dao 2.0 A.76 XLM-RoBERTa A.77 XLNet Transformer models: an introduction and catalog Xavier Amatriain xavier@amatriain.net Ananth Sankar ansankar@linkedin.com Jie Bing jbing@linkedin.com Praveen Kumar Bodigutla pbodigutla@linkedin.com Timothy J. Hazen thazen@linkedin.com Michaeel Kazi mkazi@linkedin.com Abstract In the past few years we have seen the meteoric appearance of dozens of foundation models of the Transformer family, all of which have memorable and sometimes funny, but not self-explanatory, names. The goal of this paper is to offer a somewhat comprehensive but simple catalog and classification of the most popular Transformer models. The paper also includes an introduction to the most important aspects and innovations in Transformer models. Our catalog will include models that are trained using self-supervised learning (e.g., BERT or GPT3) as well as those that are further trained using a human-in-the-loop (e.g. the InstructGPT model used by ChatGPT). Contents 1 Introduction: What are Transformers 1.1 Encoder/Decoder architecture 1.2 Attention 1.3 Foundation vs Fine-tuned models 1.4 The Impact of Transformers 1.5 A Note on Diffusion models 2 The Transformers Catalog 2.1 Features of a Transformer 2.1.1 Pretraining Architecture 2.1.2 Pretraining or Finetuning Task 2.1.3 Application 2.2 Catalog table 2.3 Family Tree 2.4 Chronological timeline A Catalog List A.1 ALBERT A.2 AlexaTM 20B A.3 Alpaca A.4 AlphaFold A.5 Anthropic Assistant A.6 BART A.7 BERT A.8 Big Bird A.9 BlenderBot3 A.10 BLOOM A.11 ChatGPT A.12 Chinchilla A.13 CLIP A.14 CM3 A.15 CTRL A.16 DALL-E A.17 DALL-E 2 A.18 DeBERTa A.19 Decision Transformers A.20 DialoGPT A.21 DistilBERT A.22 DQ-BART A.23 Dolly A.24 E5 A.25 ELECTRA A.26 ERNIE A.27 Flamingo A.28 Flan-T5 A.29 Flan-PaLM A.30 Galactica A.31 Gato A.32 GLaM A.33 GLIDE A.34 GLM A.35 Global Context ViT A.36 Gopher A.37 GopherCite A.38 GPT A.39 GPT-2 A.40 GPT-3 A.41 GPT-3.5 A.42 GPT-J A.43 GPT-Neo A.44 GPT-NeoX-20B A.45 HTLM A.46 Imagen A.47 InstructGPT A.48 InstructOR A.49 Jurassic-1 A.50 LAMDA A.51 LLaMA A.52 mBART A.53 Megatron A.54 Minerva A.55 MT-NLG (Megatron TuringNLG) A.56 OpenAssistant LLaMa A.57 OPT A.58 PalM A.59 Pegasus A.60 Pythia A.61 RoBERTa A.62 SeeKer A.63 Sparrow A.64 StableDiffusion A.65 Swin Transformer A.66 Switch A.67 T0 A.68 T5 A.69 Trajectory Transformers A.70 Transformer XL A.71 Turing-NLG A.72 UL2 A.73 Vicuna A.74 ViT A.75 Wu Dao 2.0 A.76 XLM-RoBERTa A.77 XLNet 1 Introduction: What are Transformers Transformers are a class of deep learning models that are defined by some architectural traits. They were first introduced in the now famous "Attention is All you Need" paper (and associated blog post 1 1 1 https://ai.googleblog.com/2017/08/transformer-novel-neural-network.html ) by Google researchers in 2017 ( ?). The paper has accumulated a whopping 38k citations in only 5 years. The original Transformer architecture is a specific instance of the encoder-decoder models ( ?) 2 2 2 https://machinelearningmastery.com/encoder-decoder-long-short-term-memory-networks/ that had become popular just over the 2–3 years prior. Up until that point however, attention was just one of the mechanisms used by these models, which were mostly based on LSTM (Long Short Term Memory) ( ?) and other RNN (Recurrent Neural Networks) ( ?) variations. The key insight of the Transformers paper, as the title implies, was that attention could be used as the only mechanism to derive dependencies between input and output. The input to the Transformer is a sequence of tokens. The output of the encoder is a fixed-dimensional representation for each of the tokens along with a separate embedding for the sequence as a whole. The decoder takes the output of the encoder as input, and spits out a sequence of tokens as its output. In natural language processing (NLP), the tokens can be words or subwords. Subwords are used in all popular Transformer NLP models because they enable us to address the out-of-vocabulary (OOV) issue that is inherent in a word-based system. For simplicity, we will use the term "token" to refer to the items in the input and output sequences, understanding that these tokens are subwords for NLP systems. When Transformers are used for processing images or video, the tokens can represent sub-images or objects. Since the publication of the paper, popular models like BERT and GPT have used only the encoder or decoder aspects of the original architecture. The core commonality of these models is, thus, not the encoder-decoder aspect, but, rather, the architecture of the individual layers in the encoders and decoders. The layer architecture of Transformers is based on a self-attention mechanism and a feed-forward layer, the core aspect of this being that each input token flows through the layers in its own path, while, at the same time, being directly dependent on every other token in the input sequence. This enables parallel and direct computation of contextual token representations which was previously not possible with sequential models like RNNs. It is beyond the scope of this paper to go into all the details of the Transformer architecture. For that, we will refer you to the original paper ( ?) or to The Illustrated Transformer 3 3 3 https://jalammar.github.io/illustrated-transformer/ post. That being said, we will briefly describe the most important aspects since we will be referring to them in the catalog below. Let’s start with the basic architectural diagram from the original paper, and describe some of the components. 1.1 Encoder/Decoder architecture A generic encoder/decoder architecture (see Figure 1 ) is composed of two models. The encoder takes the input and encodes it into a fixed-length vector. The decoder takes that vector and decodes it into the output sequence. The encoder and decoder are jointly trained to maximize the conditional log-likelihood of the output given the input. Once trained, the encoder/decoder can generate an output given an input sequence or can score a pair of input/output sequences. Figure 1: Transformer Architecture from ( ?) In the case of the original Transformer architecture, both encoder and decoder had 6 identical layers. In each of those 6 layers the Encoder had two sub layers: a multi-head self attention layer, and a simple feed forward network. The self attention layer computes the output representation of each of its input tokens based on all the input tokens .
